Reliability and validity of commercially available wearable devices for measurin
authors: Fuller D, Colwell E, Low J, Orychock K, Tobin MA, Simango B, Buote R, Van Heerden D, Luan H, Cullen K, Slade L, Woodruff SJ
headline: Apple and Samsung had highest validity for step count; Apple, Fitbit, and Garmin showed acceptable heart rate accuracy. Energy expenditure accuracy was poor across all devices.

label: Comparison of raw accelerometry data from ActiGraph, Apple Watch, Garmin, and Fi
authors: White JW III, Finnegan OL, Tindall N, Nelakuditi S, Amick RZ, Nocera JR, Bhattacharya S
headline: Wrist-worn consumer devices showed poor agreement with research-grade ActiGraph for raw acceleration. Device-specific APIs yielded inconsistent data formats and sampling rates.

label: Heart rate measurement accuracy of Fitbit Charge 4 and Samsung Galaxy Watch Acti
authors: Nissen M, Slim S, Jager K, Flaucher M, Huebner H, Danter L, Peter K, Fuchs J, Roeber D, Muenscher A, Kesztyues T, Weippert M, Wolf KH, Blau A, Kisters R, Fiss G, Gjoreski H, Karanakov M, Gramatikov B, Gjoreski M
headline: Fitbit Charge 4 showed MAE of 5.11 bpm and Samsung Galaxy Watch Active2 showed 4.67 bpm vs ECG; both acceptable for continuous monitoring but less accurate during high-intensity activity.

label: Heart rate detection by Fitbit ChargeHR: A validation study versus portable poly
authors: Benedetti D, Olcese U, Frumento P, Bazzani A, Bruno S, d'Ascanio P, Maestri M, Bonanni E, Faraguna U
headline: Fitbit ChargeHR showed good accuracy for heart rate during sleep (MAE 2.4 bpm) via API-accessed raw data, but accuracy degraded during movement and wake periods.

label: Performance of a commercial multi-sensor wearable (Fitbit Charge HR) in measurin
authors: Godino JG, Wing D, de Zambotti M, Baker FC, Bagot K, Inkelis S, Pautz C, Ghai S, Hellemann G, Allen N, Whalen C, Colrain IM
headline: Fitbit Charge HR overestimated sleep duration and underestimated wake after sleep onset in children. Step count accuracy was acceptable during structured activity but poor during free-living condition

label: Fitbit's accuracy to measure short bouts of stepping and sedentary behaviour: va
authors: Delobelle J, Lebuf E, Van Dyck D, Compernolle S, De Bourdeaudhuij I, Cardon G, Crombez G, Van Hoye K, Mertens L, Verloigne M, Van Lippevelde W, Deforche B, D'Haese S, Poppe L
headline: Fitbit showed 78% sensitivity for detecting short stepping bouts (>30s) but poor specificity for sedentary behavior classification. Fitbit API data extraction via Fitabase was reliable.

label: Understanding activity and physiology at scale: the Apple Heart & Movement Study
authors: Truslow J, Spillane A, Lin H, Cyr K, Ullal A, Arnold T, Huang J, McCulloch M, Desai SA, Tison GH
headline: Demonstrated scalable collection of HealthKit data (activity, heart rate, ECG) from >200,000 participants. Data quality varied by demographic factors, with lower compliance in older adults.

label: Design and methods of the Apple Women's Health Study: a digital longitudinal coh
authors: Mahalingaiah S, Fruh V, Rodriguez E, Vehik K, Bhargava R, McCabe CF, Johnson N, Brawarsky P, Ackerly C, Williams PL, Missmer SA, Jukic AMZ, Cramer DW, Hauser R, Terry KL
venue: American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
headline: Used ResearchKit for consent/surveys and HealthKit for menstrual tracking. Demonstrates scalability but participants skewed young, white, and college-educated.

label: Validity and reliability of the Apple Health app on iPhone for measuring gait pa
authors: Werner C, Hezel N, Dongus F, Spielmann J, Mayer J, Becker S, Rapp M
headline: Apple Health app demonstrated excellent test-retest reliability (ICC > 0.90) for step count and walking distance but underestimated gait speed by 8-12% across all age groups.

label: Feasibility, adherence and usability of an observational digital health study bu
authors: Brady B, Zhou S, Ashworth D, Zheng L, Dennis S, Rutter A, Gibb M, Rahimi A, Houssami N, Butow P, Dhillon HM, Delaney GP
headline: ResearchKit study showed 72% adherence at 3 months but significant drop-off among participants aged >65. HealthKit passive data completeness was higher than survey-based active data.

label: Validation of Garmin and Polar devices for continuous heart rate monitoring duri
authors: Merrigan JJ, Stovall JH, Stone JD, Stephenson MD, Feairheller DL, Galster SM, Hagen JA
venue: Measurement in Physical Education and Exercise Science
headline: Garmin Fenix 6 showed acceptable HR accuracy during steady-state (MAE 4.1 bpm) but errors increased during HIIT and resistance exercises (MAE > 12 bpm).

label: Continuous monitoring of heart rate variability in free-living conditions using
authors: Gaur P, Temple DS, Hegarty-Craver M, Sazonov E, Presti P, Bunea I, Ifteni P
headline: Garmin Fenix 6 provided consistent HRV data in free-living conditions but had 23% data loss during sleep. Garmin API required proprietary Health API with limited historical data retrieval.

label: Limiting racial disparities and bias for wearable devices in health science rese
authors: Colvonen PJ, DeYoung PN, Bosompra NO, Owens RL
headline: Wearable devices systematically underperform in individuals with darker skin tones, higher BMI, and older age. PPG-based heart rate and SpO2 measurements show significant bias across demographic group

label: Demographic imbalances resulting from the bring-your-own-device study design
authors: Cho PJ, Yi J, Ho E, Shandhi MMH, Dinh Y, Patil S, Ventrella J, Klein L, Singh K, Dunn J
headline: BYOD studies disproportionately enroll iPhone users (78% vs 55% population share) and higher-income participants, creating systematic demographic bias in digital health research.

label: Patterns of ownership and usage of wearable devices in the United States, 2020-2
authors: Nagappan A, Krasniansky A, Knowles M
headline: US wearable ownership reached 35% by 2022 with significant disparities: 48% high-income vs 18% low-income, 42% white vs 28% Black and 26% Hispanic.

label: Assessment of ownership of smart devices and the acceptability of digital health
authors: Shandhi MMH, Singh K, Janson N, Ashar P, Cho PJ, Dinh Y, Patil S, Ventrella J, Klein L, Lee S, Dunn J
headline: Only 40% of US adults own a smart wearable. Willingness to share data: 67% with researchers but only 32% with insurers. Privacy concerns were the top barrier for non-owners.

label: Wearable devices in healthcare: Privacy and information security issues
headline: Most wearable devices and cloud platforms lack adequate encryption and consent mechanisms. Only 23% of fitness tracker apps had comprehensive privacy policies.

label: Does deidentification of data from wearable devices give us a false sense of sec
authors: Chikwetu L, Miao Y, Woldetensae MK, Bell D, Goldenholz DM, Dunn J
headline: Standard deidentification is insufficient for wearable data. Re-identification rates of 60-96% using step count and heart rate time series alone. Regulatory frameworks inadequately address re-identifi

label: Ethical considerations for the use of consumer wearables in health research
authors: Sui A, Sui W, Liu S, Rhodes R
headline: Consumer wearable platforms have complex data sharing agreements permitting commercial use of research data. Most studies using wearable APIs do not adequately inform participants about third-party da

label: Data interoperability for ambulatory monitoring of cardiovascular disease: a sci
authors: Armoundas AA, Ahmad FS, Bennett DA, Breathett K, Chung MK, Dimarco JP, Elkind MS, Gluckman TJ, Goldberger ZD, Kuzniewicz M, Maron DJ, Martinez MW, Tereshchenko LG, Tison GH, Turakhia MP
headline: Lack of interoperability between Apple HealthKit, Google Fit, Fitbit, and Garmin is a major barrier to clinical adoption. FHIR-based standards and Health Connect are promising but incomplete solutions

label: A scalable, secure, and interoperable platform for deep data-driven health manag
authors: Bahmani A, Alavi A, Buergel T, Upadhyayula S, Wang Q, Chatterjee R, Srinivasan S, Ravichandran S, Li Y, Huo S, Rodriguez B, Jain K, Hemberg M, Bhatt DL, Dunn J
headline: The Personal Health Dashboard platform integrated data from Apple HealthKit, Google Fit, Fitbit, Garmin, and EHRs. Custom adapters were required for each platform due to incompatible data schemas and

label: Wearable sensors with possibilities for data exchange: Analyzing status and need
authors: Muzny M, Henriksen A, Giordanengo A, Muzik J, Hartvigsen G, Torsvik T, Grimsgaard S, Hopstock LA, Strandberg RB
headline: Only 38% of consumer wearable devices offered API access for research. Fitbit and Garmin provided richer data via APIs but with rate limits. Apple HealthKit and Google Fit acted primarily as data aggr

label: Challenges and recommendations for wearable devices in digital health: Data qual
authors: Canali S, Schiaffonati V, Aliverti A
headline: Four major challenges: (1) poor data quality from consumer-grade sensors, (2) lack of cross-platform interoperability, (3) demographic bias in training data causing algorithmic fairness issues, (4) in

label: Data quality evaluation in wearable monitoring
authors: Bottcher S, Vieluf S, Bruno E, Joseph B, Epitashvili N, Marschitz T, Reinsberger C
headline: Proposed a standardized data quality evaluation framework. Found 15-30% of wearable data in free-living conditions is corrupted by motion artifacts. PPG-based sensors were most affected.

label: On general data protection regulation vulnerabilities and privacy issues, for we
headline: Security analysis of 12 fitness trackers: 8 transmitted unencrypted data. Multiple platforms leaked personal identifiers through API endpoints. GDPR compliance was incomplete for most platforms.
